content:
Generate Google Gemini videos from a sentence, animate your images, or feed in an existing video and transform it. Working code, real costs, and the one strict rule of video editing.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The series has covered chat, images, and image editing. Today we reach the modality we promised weeks ago: video. The Gemini Videos API on Ace Data Cloud generates video through Google’s omni-flash model, and it does three distinct things: turns text into video, turns your images into video, and, the standout, takes an existing video plus a prompt and gives you back a transformed one.
Every example in this guide comes from the live documentation, including real generation times and real costs. Let’s build.
The three modes
Text to video: describe a scene, get a video. The classic.
Image to video: provide reference images and a prompt, and the model animates from them. Your product shot becomes a moving scene.
Video editing: provide an existing video (plus at least one reference image, a strict upstream requirement) and describe the change: swap the season, change the style, add or remove elements. The docs’ own example turns a sunny tropical beach into a snowfall scene while keeping the same beach, palm trees, and boats.
Your first video
The endpoint:
POST https://api.acedata.cloud/gemini/videosThree main fields: prompt (required), model (currently omni-flash, the default), and aspect_ratio (16:9 landscape or 9:16 portrait, default landscape). Optional resolution takes 720p (default) or 1080p.
python
import requestsurl = "https://api.acedata.cloud/gemini/videos"headers = {
"accept": "application/json",
"authorization": "Bearer YOUR_TOKEN",
"content-type": "application/json"
}payload = {
"prompt": "A cinematic shot of a kitten chasing a butterfly in a sunlit garden",
"model": "omni-flash",
"aspect_ratio": "16:9"
}response = requests.post(url, json=payload, headers=headers)
print(response.json())The response carries your video link in data[0].video_url, plus the useful extras: state (pending / succeeded / failed), elapsed (the docs' example took about 67 seconds), and cost, which shows exactly what the request charged. In the documented example: about 2 credits per video, roughly $0.30 at the base credit rate. Check the live pricing page for current figures.
One practical note from the docs worth highlighting: generated video and image links have a storage time limit and expire. Download your results and store them yourself as soon as you have them.
Image to video
Pass reference images in image_urls and the prompt describes the motion:
python
payload = {
"prompt": "The woman slowly turns around and smiles at the camera, gentle breeze",
"model": "omni-flash",
"aspect_ratio": "9:16",
"image_urls": [
"https://your-cdn.com/reference-image.png"
]
}This pairs naturally with the image models on the same platform: generate a still with Seedream or Nano Banana, then hand it to Gemini to bring it to life, all on one key and one balance.
Video editing: input a video, get a new one
This is the mode that feels like the future. Pass one reference video in video_urls, at least one reference image in image_urls (the strict rule: video editing requires both, or the API returns a clear parameter error), and describe the transformation:
python
payload = {
"prompt": "Turn this sunny tropical beach into a snowy winter scene, heavy snowfall and overcast sky, keep the same beach, palm trees and boat layout",
"model": "omni-flash",
"aspect_ratio": "9:16",
"resolution": "720p",
"image_urls": ["https://your-cdn.com/reference.png"],
"video_urls": ["https://your-cdn.com/original-video.mp4"],
"async": True
}Editing takes longer than generation, the documented real example ran about six and a half minutes, which is why the request above sets async: true: the API returns a task_id immediately instead of holding the connection open.
Async, callbacks, and checking on your videos
Two ways to handle the wait:
Callback: pass a callback_url and the API POSTs the finished result to your server when done.
Polling: with async: true, take the returned task_id and query the Gemini Tasks API:
POST https://api.acedata.cloud/gemini/taskspython
payload = {"id": "your-task-id-here"}While the video is rendering, state reads pending and video_url is null. When it flips to succeeded, your link is ready. This is the same free background-job pattern we covered in the Tasks article, applied to video.
Handling errors
The codes to expect: 400 for parameter problems (missing prompt, invalid aspect ratio, or video_urls without image_urls), 401 for token issues, 403 for insufficient balance or a prompt rejected by content moderation, and 500 for upstream generation failures worth retrying.
Pricing
Video generation draws from the same shared credit balance as all 200+ models on the platform, and every response shows its exact cost in the cost field, so there is never a surprise. The documented example charged about 2 credits per video. Holding $ACE applies an automatic discount of up to 20% on top of listed prices. Check the live pricing page for current rates.
Getting started
Create an Ace Data Cloud account, copy your API token from the console, and run the kitten example above. One token covers every service on the platform, and new accounts receive free starter credit, so your first videos cost nothing to try. Remember to download what you generate.
Final thoughts
The Gemini Videos API completes a pattern the platform keeps repeating: a frontier capability, wrapped in the same simple request shape, on the same key and balance as everything else. Text becomes video, images become video, and now video becomes different video. Pair it with the image models upstream and the Tasks API for job tracking, and you have a full production pipeline in a handful of HTTP requests.
Next in the series: more of the catalog, from music generation to search. Follow along so you do not miss it.
Start building at platform.acedata.cloud
